methodology: discriminating-only, 10 topics, 36 evals, ~115 assertions targeting AI failure patterns

evals.name: hardcoded-strings

assertions.text: Identifies all three strings as hardcoded and missing localization
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 1: never hardcode user-facing strings

assertions.text: Recommends String(localized:) for iOS 15+ or NSLocalizedString for older targets
assertions.rationale: Skill provides modern vs legacy API guidance

assertions.text: Provides meaningful translator comments (not empty comment: "")
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 3: always provide comments with UI context; WITHOUT often uses empty comments

evals.name: string-concatenation
evals.prompt:
What's the localization problem with this code?

```swift
let message = NSLocalizedString("hello", comment: "") + ", " + userName + "!"
```

assertions.text: Identifies concatenation as preventing word reordering for other languages
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 4: concatenation locks into English word order

assertions.text: Recommends a single format string with placeholder (e.g., String(format:) with %@)
assertions.rationale: Skill pattern: single format string allows translator reordering

assertions.text: Uses positional format specifiers (%1$@) in the fix
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 5: positional specifiers allow argument reordering; WITHOUT often uses non-positional %@

evals.name: ambiguous-english-keys
evals.prompt: I'm building an app with a menu and a garden feature. Both use the word "Water". How should I handle this in localization?

assertions.text: Recommends separate keys for different contexts (e.g., 'menu.drink.water' and 'garden.action.water')
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 2/6: same English word may need different translations

assertions.text: Provides different translator comments explaining each context
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 3: comments disambiguate for translators

assertions.text: Uses semantic dot-notation keys (not the English word as key)
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 2: semantic keys not English text; WITHOUT often uses 'Water' as key

evals.name: non-positional-specifiers

assertions.text: Identifies non-positional %@ as preventing translator reordering
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 5: Korean/Japanese/Arabic may need reversed argument order

assertions.text: Recommends %1$@ and %2$@ positional specifiers
assertions.rationale: Skill shows exact Korean reordering example as motivation

assertions.text: Provides a concrete example of how a translator would reorder (e.g., Korean, Japanese, or Arabic)
assertions.rationale: Skill gives Korean example; WITHOUT often fixes without showing why reordering matters

evals.name: russian-plural-categories

assertions.text: Identifies that Russian needs 4 categories: one, few, many, other (not just one/other)
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 8: #1 AI pluralization failure

assertions.text: Provides the correct Russian forms: one=файл, few=файла, many=файлов, other=файла
assertions.rationale: Skill's Russian plural table with all four forms

assertions.text: Explains that 'few' covers numbers ending in 2-4 (not 12-14), like 2, 3, 4, 22, 23, 24
assertions.rationale: Skill documents exact category rules; WITHOUT often states categories but not the number rules

assertions.text: Mentions the verification test set or provides specific test numbers like 1, 2, 5, 11, 21, 22

evals.name: polish-vs-russian-plurals
evals.prompt: I'm adding Polish support to my app. Can I copy the Russian plural rules? Both are Slavic languages with 4 categories.

assertions.text: States that Polish 'one' is ONLY the integer 1, while Russian 'one' includes 21, 31, 101 etc.
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 10: Polish one ≠ Russian one

assertions.text: Shows a concrete example: number 21 maps to 'one' in Russian but 'many' in Polish
assertions.rationale: Skill gives the 21 example as the clearest difference

assertions.text: Warns against copying plural logic between Slavic languages
assertions.rationale: Skill explicitly warns about cross-language copying; WITHOUT often says 'same 4 categories'

evals.name: count-ternary-plural
evals.prompt:
What's wrong with this pluralization approach?

```swift
let text = count == 1 ? "\(count) item" : "\(count) items"
```

assertions.text: Identifies the ternary as breaking for languages with more than 2 plural forms
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 9: never use count == 1 ternary for plurals

assertions.text: Recommends .stringsdict or String Catalog plural variations
assertions.rationale: Skill redirects to system plural handling

assertions.text: Names specific languages affected (Russian, Polish, Arabic, or similar)
assertions.rationale: Skill lists affected language families; WITHOUT often just says 'some languages'

evals.name: arabic-plural-categories
evals.prompt: I'm adding Arabic localization. How many plural categories does Arabic need?

assertions.text: States Arabic needs all 6 CLDR categories: zero, one, two, few, many, other
assertions.rationale: Skill documents Arabic as the most complex plural system

assertions.text: Explains that 'two' is specifically for the number 2 (dual form)
assertions.rationale: Arabic dual is unique; WITHOUT often misses the 'two' category

assertions.text: Provides example number ranges for at least few and many categories
assertions.rationale: Skill's Arabic rules table with number ranges per category

evals.name: text-string-variable
evals.prompt:
Why doesn't this localization work?

```swift
let key = "greeting_key"
Text(key)
```

assertions.text: Identifies that String variable uses StringProtocol overload, not LocalizedStringKey
assertions.rationale: Skill documents the literal vs variable trap

assertions.text: Shows the fix: Text(LocalizedStringKey(key))
assertions.rationale: Skill provides explicit conversion for dynamic keys

assertions.text: Explains that only string LITERALS trigger localization, not variables
assertions.rationale: Skill's core SwiftUI trap; WITHOUT often just shows the fix without explaining why

evals.name: verbatim-missing
evals.prompt:
I see version strings and URLs appearing in my String Catalog. How do I prevent that?

```swift
Text("v2.0.1")
Text("https://example.com/terms")
```

assertions.text: Recommends Text(verbatim:) for non-localizable strings
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 11: verbatim prevents extraction

assertions.text: Explains that Text() with literals auto-extracts to String Catalog
assertions.rationale: Skill documents the auto-extraction behavior

assertions.text: Mentions #if DEBUG for debug-only text as another prevention technique
assertions.rationale: Skill covers both verbatim and conditional compilation; WITHOUT often only shows verbatim

evals.name: package-bundle-missing
evals.prompt:
My Swift Package's localized strings aren't appearing — it shows the key instead of the translation:

```swift
// Inside MyPackage/Sources/FeatureAuth/LoginView.swift
Text("login_title")
String(localized: "login_subtitle")
```

assertions.text: Identifies missing bundle: .module parameter
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 14: package strings default to Bundle.main without bundle:

assertions.text: Shows the fix with bundle: .module on both Text and String(localized:)
assertions.rationale: Skill shows both SwiftUI and String(localized:) fixes

assertions.text: Mentions that Package.swift needs defaultLocalization: "en"
assertions.rationale: Skill's enterprise patterns require this setting; WITHOUT often misses it

evals.name: interpolation-type-trap
evals.prompt:
Why does my Image disappear in this localized string?

```swift
let label = "Delete \(Image(systemName: \"trash\"))"
Text(label)
```

assertions.text: Identifies that Swift infers String type, using DefaultStringInterpolation
assertions.rationale: Skill documents the interpolation type trap

assertions.text: Shows the fix: explicit LocalizedStringKey type annotation
assertions.rationale: Skill's exact fix: let label: LocalizedStringKey = ...

assertions.text: Explains that LocalizedStringKey has custom StringInterpolation for Image/Date/Text
assertions.rationale: Skill explains the mechanism; WITHOUT often just shows the fix

evals.name: nslocalized-interpolation
evals.prompt:
Is this correct?

```swift
let message = NSLocalizedString("Hello \(userName)", comment: "Greeting")
```

assertions.text: Identifies that interpolation creates a unique key per value ("Hello Alice", "Hello Bob")
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 15: NSLocalizedString with interpolation creates dynamic keys

assertions.text: Recommends String(format:) with a static key
assertions.rationale: Skill's fix pattern: static key + format

evals.name: en-us-posix-missing

assertions.text: Identifies missing en_US_POSIX locale as the cause
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 16: most damaging date bug

assertions.text: Explains that Buddhist calendar users get wrong year (2568 instead of 2025)
assertions.rationale: Skill provides specific Buddhist calendar example; WITHOUT often just says 'some calendars'

assertions.text: Explains that 12-hour time users break HH:mm parsing
assertions.rationale: Skill documents both failure modes; WITHOUT often only mentions one

evals.name: custom-dateformat-display
evals.prompt:
Is this the right way to display a date to the user?

```swift
let formatter = DateFormatter()
formatter.dateFormat = "MM/dd/yyyy"
label.text = formatter.string(from: date)
```

assertions.text: Identifies MM/dd/yyyy as US-specific format that's wrong for most of the world
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 17: never custom dateFormat for user-facing dates

assertions.text: Recommends .dateStyle/.timeStyle or Date.formatted() (iOS 15+)
assertions.rationale: Skill provides multiple modern alternatives

assertions.text: Mentions setLocalizedDateFormatFromTemplate() for custom patterns
assertions.rationale: Skill's template approach auto-reorders per locale; WITHOUT often only mentions dateStyle

evals.name: yyyy-vs-YYYY
evals.prompt:
My date format shows wrong year near January 1st:

```swift
formatter.dateFormat = "YYYY-MM-dd"
```

assertions.text: Identifies YYYY as ISO week-numbering year, not calendar year
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 18: YYYY vs yyyy

assertions.text: Explains the specific scenario: Dec 30, 2024 shows as 2025 with YYYY
assertions.rationale: Skill provides concrete example of the year boundary bug

assertions.text: Also warns about DD (day of year) vs dd (day of month)
assertions.rationale: Skill pairs both format specifier traps; WITHOUT often misses DD

evals.name: currency-interpolation
evals.prompt:
How should I display prices in my app?

```swift
let priceText = "$\(product.price)"
```

assertions.text: Identifies string interpolation as wrong for currencies (symbol position, decimals, grouping vary)
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 20: never interpolate currency

assertions.text: Recommends .formatted(.currency(code:)) for iOS 15+ or NumberFormatter
assertions.rationale: Skill provides both modern and legacy formatters

assertions.text: Shows how the same amount renders differently in different locales (e.g., US vs Germany vs France)
assertions.rationale: Skill's currency table shows $1,234.56 vs 1.234,56 $ vs 1 234,56 $

evals.name: number-digits-locale
evals.prompt: My app shows Western Arabic digits (1, 2, 3) even for Arabic locale users. How do I fix this?

assertions.text: Identifies String(format:) as always outputting Western digits
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 21: String(format:) ignores locale

assertions.text: Recommends String.localizedStringWithFormat() or .formatted()
assertions.rationale: Skill's locale-aware formatting APIs

evals.name: left-right-constraints
evals.prompt:
What's the localization issue with this layout?

```swift
NSLayoutConstraint.activate([
    label.leftAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.leftAnchor, constant: 16),
    label.rightAnchor.constraint(equalTo: view.rightAnchor, constant: -16)
])
```

assertions.text: Identifies left/right as never flipping for RTL languages (Arabic, Hebrew)
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 22: always leading/trailing

assertions.text: Replaces with leadingAnchor/trailingAnchor
assertions.rationale: Skill's exact fix pattern

assertions.text: Mentions that this affects 400M+ Arabic/Hebrew users or provides RTL user count
assertions.rationale: Skill emphasizes the population impact; WITHOUT often just shows the fix

evals.name: text-alignment-left
evals.prompt: Should I use .left or .natural for text alignment in a localized app?

assertions.text: Recommends .natural and explains it auto-adapts to RTL
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 24: .natural not .left

assertions.text: Explains that .left stays left regardless of language direction
assertions.rationale: Skill documents why .left is wrong for RTL

evals.name: force-ltr-exceptions
evals.prompt: I'm building an RTL-compatible app. Should ALL content flip to RTL?

assertions.text: Lists exceptions: phone numbers, playback controls, progress bars
assertions.rationale: Skill documents inherently LTR content types

assertions.text: Shows semanticContentAttribute = .forceLeftToRight (UIKit) or .environment(\.layoutDirection, .leftToRight) (SwiftUI)
assertions.rationale: Skill provides both UIKit and SwiftUI force-LTR patterns

assertions.text: Mentions directional images (arrows, chevrons) should flip but logos should not
assertions.rationale: Skill's image flipping guidance; WITHOUT often misses image considerations

evals.name: fixed-width-localized-text
evals.prompt:
My German translations are getting truncated:

```swift
Text(localizedString).frame(width: 120)
```

assertions.text: Identifies fixed width as the problem (German averages 30% longer than English)
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 25: no fixed widths on localized text

assertions.text: Recommends flexible constraints (maxWidth: .infinity or no width constraint)
assertions.rationale: Skill's fix: flexible Auto Layout

assertions.text: Mentions Double Length pseudolanguage for testing text expansion
assertions.rationale: Skill's testing reference covers pseudolanguage testing; WITHOUT rarely mentions it

evals.name: hardcoded-accessibility-labels
evals.prompt:
Are these accessibility labels correct?

```swift
closeButton.accessibilityLabel = "Close"
slider.accessibilityValue = "50 percent"
image.accessibilityLabel = "User profile photo"
```

assertions.text: Identifies all three as hardcoded English — broken for non-English VoiceOver users
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 26: localize all accessibility labels

assertions.text: Shows the fix using String(localized:) with accessibility-specific comments
assertions.rationale: Skill's localized accessibility pattern

assertions.text: Confirms accessibilityIdentifier should NOT be localized
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 27: identifier is for test automation; WITHOUT sometimes confuses the two

evals.name: swiftui-accessibility-localization
evals.prompt:
How do I localize accessibility labels in SwiftUI? I have:

```swift
Button(action: share) { Image(systemName: "square.and.arrow.up") }
    .accessibilityLabel("Share")
```

assertions.text: Explains that SwiftUI .accessibilityLabel with a string literal auto-localizes (it's a LocalizedStringKey)
assertions.rationale: Skill notes SwiftUI auto-localization from literals

assertions.text: But warns that the key must exist in the String Catalog / .strings file
assertions.rationale: Auto-localization only works if the translation exists

assertions.text: Recommends using a semantic key with comment for translation context
assertions.rationale: Skill: semantic keys with comments for translators; WITHOUT often keeps English word as key

evals.name: xcstrings-validation
evals.prompt: I have a large .xcstrings file with 500+ keys. How do I check if all translations are complete and plurals are correct?

assertions.text: Recommends using the xcstrings_tool.py validate command (or similar programmatic approach)
assertions.rationale: Skill bundles scripts for large file operations

assertions.text: Mentions checking for missing translations (state = 'new'), stale entries, and empty values
assertions.rationale: Skill's validation checks all three categories

assertions.text: Mentions auditing plural completeness per language
assertions.rationale: Skill's audit-plurals command checks CLDR requirements; WITHOUT rarely mentions automated plural auditing

evals.name: xcstrings-merge-conflicts
evals.prompt: My team keeps getting merge conflicts in our Localizable.xcstrings file. What's the best approach?

assertions.text: Explains that .xcstrings is a single large JSON file where Git text merge fails
assertions.rationale: Skill documents the merge conflict problem

assertions.text: Recommends splitting strings across multiple catalogs or Swift Packages
assertions.rationale: Skill's mitigation: split by feature/module

assertions.text: Mentions the comment order randomization Xcode bug as another source of false diffs
assertions.rationale: Skill documents this known bug; WITHOUT rarely mentions it

evals.name: xcstrings-plural-without-number
evals.prompt: I need to localize 'Day' / 'Days' without showing the count. Can String Catalogs handle this?

assertions.text: States that String Catalogs CANNOT handle pluralization without a visible number
assertions.rationale: Skill documents this limitation explicitly

assertions.text: Recommends .stringsdict as the fallback for this specific case
assertions.rationale: Skill identifies .stringsdict as still required for this edge case; WITHOUT often doesn't mention the limitation

evals.name: xcstrings-migration
evals.prompt: I have an existing project with .strings and .stringsdict files. How do I migrate to String Catalogs?

assertions.text: Mentions Xcode's built-in migration: right-click .strings → Migrate to String Catalog
assertions.rationale: Skill documents the migration path

assertions.text: Warns that .strings and .xcstrings CANNOT coexist with the same table name
assertions.rationale: Skill's coexistence warning; WITHOUT often misses this constraint

assertions.text: Migration must be complete per table
assertions.rationale: Skill requires complete migration per table

evals.name: package-default-localization
evals.prompt: My Swift Package's localized strings work in the main app but not when imported as a dependency in another project. What's wrong?

assertions.text: Checks for defaultLocalization in Package.swift
assertions.rationale: Skill's enterprise patterns require this setting

assertions.text: Checks for bundle: .module in localization calls
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 14: most common enterprise bug

assertions.text: Mentions that .lproj directories must be in the package's Resources folder
assertions.rationale: Skill documents the directory structure; WITHOUT often misses resource paths

evals.name: white-label-localization
evals.prompt: We're building a white-label app with 3 different brand variants. Each brand needs different marketing text but shares most UI strings. What's the architecture?

assertions.text: Describes at least 2 of: multiple Xcode targets, runtime bundle loading, or OTA localization
assertions.rationale: Skill documents all three architectures

assertions.text: Recommends a shared catalog for common UI strings and per-brand catalogs for marketing text
assertions.rationale: Skill's split pattern for white-label

assertions.text: Mentions BrandManager or similar runtime bundle resolution pattern
assertions.rationale: Skill's BrandManager pattern; WITHOUT often suggests only target-based approach

evals.name: pseudolanguage-testing
evals.prompt: How do I test my iOS app's localization without actual translations?

assertions.text: Lists Xcode pseudolanguages: Double Length, Right to Left, Accented, Bounded
assertions.rationale: Skill's testing reference covers all four

assertions.text: Explains what each pseudolanguage tests (expansion, RTL, hardcoded strings, truncation)
assertions.rationale: Skill maps each pseudolanguage to its testing purpose

assertions.text: Mentions -NSShowNonLocalizedStrings YES launch argument
assertions.rationale: Skill documents this flag; WITHOUT often misses it

evals.name: locale-test-automation
evals.prompt: How do I write automated tests that verify localization works correctly for Russian?

assertions.text: Shows launch arguments for forcing locale: -AppleLocale ru_RU and -AppleLanguages (ru)
assertions.rationale: Skill's locale-specific test pattern

assertions.text: Uses accessibilityIdentifier (not accessibilityLabel) for element lookup
assertions.rationale: Skill rule: identifier for tests, label for users; WITHOUT often uses label for lookup

assertions.text: Mentions testing plural forms with the verification number set
assertions.rationale: Skill's test set [0,1,2,3,4,5,10,11...]; WITHOUT rarely suggests specific test numbers

evals.name: locale-change-notification
evals.prompt: My app shows stale date formats after the user changes their language in Settings without restarting the app. How do I fix this?

assertions.text: Recommends observing NSLocale.currentLocaleDidChangeNotification
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 30: invalidate cached formatters on locale change

assertions.text: Shows invalidation of cached DateFormatters/NumberFormatters
assertions.rationale: Skill's formatter cache invalidation pattern

assertions.text: Explains the scenario: user changes locale while app is backgrounded
assertions.rationale: Skill documents this runtime behavior; WITHOUT often suggests app restart instead

evals.name: enum-rawvalue-localization
evals.prompt:
My tab bar shows raw enum values instead of translated text:

```swift
enum Tab: String {
    case home, search, profile
}

Text(tab.rawValue)
```

assertions.text: Identifies that rawValue is a String variable — not a literal — so no localization occurs
assertions.rationale: Skill's enum rawValue trap

assertions.text: Recommends adding a displayName computed property with LocalizedStringKey
assertions.rationale: Skill's exact pattern: switch with localized strings

evals.name: custom-view-string-parameter

assertions.text: Identifies that String parameter prevents LocalizedStringKey auto-localization
assertions.rationale: Skill rule 13: accept LocalizedStringKey for user-facing text

assertions.text: Recommends changing parameter to LocalizedStringKey
assertions.rationale: Skill's fix: let title: LocalizedStringKey

evals.name: date-parsing-buddhist-calendar

assertions.text: Identifies that Thai locale uses Buddhist calendar by default (year = Gregorian + 543)
assertions.rationale: Skill's en_US_POSIX motivation includes Buddhist calendar

assertions.text: Distinguishes: for API output use en_US_POSIX locale, for display use .dateStyle
assertions.rationale: Skill's parsing vs display decision tree

assertions.text: For display to Thai users, the Buddhist year IS correct — only server communication needs Gregorian
assertions.rationale: Skill nuance: don't force Gregorian on user-facing display; WITHOUT often applies POSIX to everything
